content:
Formal hypothesis testing crystallized in the early 20th century from **Ronald Fisher's** work on p-values and experimental design, and **Jerzy Neyman** and **Egon Pearson's** framework pairing null/alternative hypotheses with Type I/II error rates and decision rules.

Fisher emphasized calculating how extreme data are under a null model; Neyman–Pearson formalized accept/reject regions and long-run error control.

**Student's t-test** (William Sealy Gosset, publishing as "Student," 1908) addressed small-sample inference when population variance is unknown—still the workhorse for comparing means.

Later developments—likelihood ratio tests, nonparametric methods (Wilcoxon, Mann–Whitney), and multiple-comparison corrections (Bonferroni, Benjamini–Hochberg)—extended the core logic.

In modern data science and ML, the same Fisher/Neyman–Pearson vocabulary underlies A/B testing platforms, clinical trials, and benchmark significance claims—even as practitioners increasingly emphasize effect sizes, Bayesian methods, and estimation over binary significance.

timeline.label: 1908 — Student's t
timeline.description: Gosset derives the t-distribution for small-sample mean tests when σ is unknown.

timeline.label: 1920s–1930s — Fisher and Neyman–Pearson
timeline.description: p-values, null hypotheses, and formal Type I/II error frameworks shape modern inference.

timeline.label: Post-war applied statistics
timeline.description: Industrial quality control and clinical trials standardize hypothesis testing in decision pipelines.

timeline.label: ML era
timeline.description: Online A/B tests and benchmark comparisons reuse classical tests; debates on p-hacking and reproducibility drive pre-registration and CI-first reporting.
